She invented the riddling table around 1816 to clarify champagne, rushed the 1811 comet vintage through blockades to Russia in 1814, and produced an early blended rosé in 1818, building one of the region's great commercial empires under the 'Veuve' (widow) name. The house passed through the Werlé family era, registered its yellow label in 1877, and joined what became LVMH in 1987. Its cellars occupy ancient Gallo-Roman chalk pits, and its La Grande Dame cuvée and women-in-business awards keep the founder's name at the center of the brand.
facts: Madame Clicquot ran the house for six decades, one of the first women to lead a champagne business.
The riddling table she devised around 1816 remains the basis of traditional-method clarification.
The crayères — chalk quarries used as cellars — are part of a UNESCO World Heritage listing.

timeline.event: House founded by Philippe Clicquot
Widowed Barbe-Nicole Clicquot-Ponsardin takes over
Yellow label trademark registered
Becomes part of LVMH

name: Krug
description: Krug is champagne's most single-minded luxury house: every bottle it makes is treated as a prestige cuvée. Small-cask fermentation, a vast library of reserve wines and years of extra lees aging define the style — broad, vinous, endlessly layered. The multi-vintage Grande Cuvée leads the range, with Vintage, Rosé and the single-plot Clos du Mesnil and Clos d'Ambonnay above it in rarity.
history: Joseph Krug, German-born and trained at Jacquesson, founded his house in Reims in 1843 on a conviction he recorded in a cherry-red notebook in 1848: great champagne should transcend the vintage through blending across years. Five family generations upheld the doctrine — the flagship was long called Private Cuvée, relaunched as Grande Cuvée in the late 1970s under brothers Henri and Rémi Krug, who also created Clos du Mesnil (first vintage 1979). LVMH acquired the house in 1999 with sixth-generation Olivier Krug remaining as director. Cellar master Eric Lebel introduced the numbered édition system and Krug iD; Julie Cavil succeeded him in 2020 as the house's first female cellar master.
facts: Joseph Krug's 1848 notebook of blending principles is still held — and quoted — by the house.
Krug ferments every parcel separately in small 205-litre used oak casks.
Clos du Mesnil comes from a 1.84-hectare walled chardonnay plot enclosed since 1698.

timeline.event: Joseph Krug founds the house in Reims
Grande Cuvée relaunches the flagship multi-vintage blend
LVMH acquires Krug
Julie Cavil becomes cellar master

name: Freixenet
description: Freixenet is the world's largest producer of traditional-method sparkling wine, headquartered in Sant Sadurní d'Anoia, the capital of cava. Its frosted-glass bottles — white Carta Nevada and black Cordon Negro — carried Spanish bubbles to every corner of the global market, offering bottle-fermented sparkle from native macabeo, xarel·lo and parellada at everyday prices.
history: The house united two Penedès winegrowing families when Pedro Ferrer Bosch of the La Freixeneda estate — 'el Freixenet' — married Dolores Sala Vivé of the Casa Sala wine firm; they sold their first sparkling wine under the Freixenet name in 1914 and focused the business entirely on cava. Carta Nevada's frosted bottle (1941) and Cordon Negro's matte black one (1974) became category icons, boosted by decades of famous Christmas television advertising in Spain. The Ferrer family expanded internationally with cavas, Sekt and still-wine estates across three continents. In 2018 Germany's Henkell & Co. acquired majority control, creating Henkell Freixenet, the world's biggest sparkling-wine group, with the historic cellars beneath Sant Sadurní still at its heart.
facts: Freixenet means 'ash grove' in Catalan, from the family estate La Freixeneda.
Its Christmas TV adverts, running since the 1970s, are a Spanish cultural institution.
The company's cellars tunnel directly beneath the town of Sant Sadurní d'Anoia.

timeline.event: First sparkling wines sold under the Freixenet name
Cordon Negro launched in its black frosted bottle
Henkell & Co. takes majority control, forming Henkell Freixenet

name: La Marca
description: La Marca is a consortium of winegrowing cooperatives in the province of Treviso, pooling thousands of grower families across the Prosecco DOC heartland. Its powder-blue-labeled prosecco, tank-fermented from glera grapes for maximum freshness, became one of the best-selling sparkling wine imports in the United States and a fixture of the spritz era.
history: Founded in 1968 in Oderzo as a union of cooperative wineries, La Marca takes its name from La Marca Trevigiana, the historical name for the Treviso territory. For decades it worked as a growers' organization supplying the regional wine trade; its transformation came with prosecco's global boom after the 2009 reorganization of the Prosecco DOC, when the export brand — marketed in the United States in partnership with E. & J. Gallo — rode the category from obscurity to ubiquity. Today the consortium unites a large share of Treviso's cooperative growers, balancing industrial scale with the DOC's tank-method template of light, floral, fruit-first sparkling wine.
facts: The consortium represents thousands of grower families across the Treviso plain.
'La Marca Trevigiana' is the centuries-old name for the March of Treviso.
Its US rise paralleled the prosecco boom that made the category outsell champagne by volume.

timeline.event: Cooperative consortium founded in Oderzo
Prosecco DOC reorganized; export brand accelerates

name: Brouwerij der Sint-Sixtusabdij (Westvleteren)
description: The brewery of Saint-Sixtus Abbey in Westvleteren is the smallest and most uncompromising of the Trappist producers: the monks brew three beers — Blond, 8 and 12 — only in the quantities needed to sustain their community, and sell them only at the abbey by reservation. That refusal to scale, combined with the 12's perennial 'best beer in the world' rankings, made a modest monastic economy into beer's greatest cult.
history: The abbey was founded in 1831 by monks from the Catsberg hermitage, and brewing began in 1838. For a period through 1992, the nearby St. Bernardus brewery brewed licensed 'St. Sixtus' beers commercially, but the monks ended the arrangement and pulled all production back inside the walls, reaffirming their 1946 decision to sell only at the gate. The modern range solidified around the Blond, 8 and 12, with the 12 dating to 1940. Demand exploded in the internet ratings era, forcing a telephone-and-plate-number reservation system. The single sanctioned exception to gate-only sales was 2012's international gift-pack release, which funded urgent renovations. A handful of monks still run each brew day, assisted by a few lay workers.
facts: Beer is sold by advance reservation at the abbey, with per-car limits and a no-resale request.
Bottles carry no labels; all mandatory information is printed on the crown caps.
St. Bernardus in nearby Watou traces its recipes to a mid-century licensing deal with the abbey.

timeline.event: Saint-Sixtus Abbey founded at Westvleteren
Brewing begins inside the abbey
All brewing returns exclusively inside the abbey walls
One-time worldwide gift-pack sale funds renovations

name: Guinness (St. James's Gate Brewery)
description: Guinness's St. James's Gate brewery has brewed black beer beside the Liffey since 1759 and remains the spiritual home of stout, even as the brand is brewed under license on five continents. Once the largest brewery in the world, it pairs industrial scale with a research tradition that produced the nitrogenated draught pour, the widget and — via brewer W.S. Gosset — the Student's t-test.
history: Arthur Guinness signed his famous 9,000-year lease on a disused Dublin brewery in 1759 and shifted from ale to the London-style porters that became the house's fortune, exporting stout across the empire by the nineteenth century, when St. James's Gate ranked as the world's biggest brewery. The harp trademark arrived in 1876. Brewer Michael Ash's nitrogenation project produced Guinness Draught in 1959; the can widget followed in 1989. Guinness merged with Grand Metropolitan in 1997 to form Diageo, which invested heavily in the Dublin site and its Storehouse visitor center — Ireland's most-visited attraction — while global variants from Foreign Extra Stout to Nitro Cold Brew extend the family.
facts: Statistician W.S. Gosset invented the Student's t-test while working as a Guinness brewer, publishing under a pseudonym.
The Guinness Storehouse is Ireland's most-visited tourist attraction.
About 40% of all Guinness is brewed and drunk in Africa, where Foreign Extra Stout dominates.

timeline.event: Arthur Guinness signs the 9,000-year lease at St. James's Gate
Nitrogenated Guinness Draught invented for the bicentenary
Guinness merges into Diageo

name: Sierra Nevada Brewing Co.
description: Sierra Nevada is the archetypal American craft brewery: founded in 1980 by homebrewer Ken Grossman in Chico, California, still family-owned, and responsible for the pale ale that taught the country what Cascade hops taste like. It pairs traditionalist technique — whole-cone hops, bottle conditioning — with engineering obsession, from carbon-dioxide recovery to one of the largest private solar arrays in US brewing.
history: Ken Grossman ran a Chico homebrew shop before building a brewery by hand with co-founder Paul Camusi in 1980, welding tanks from salvaged dairy equipment; the pale ale first brewed that November became craft beer's foundational text. Growth was steady and self-funded: Bigfoot barleywine (1983), Celebration fresh-hop IPA, Torpedo and the hazy juggernaut Hazy Little Thing (2018). A second brewery in Mills River, North Carolina opened in 2014 to serve the East Coast, with a LEED Platinum rating. Grossman bought out Camusi in 1998, and his children Brian and Sierra now help lead the company, one of the largest craft brewers in the United States and a fixture of 'independent' advocacy.
facts: The first brewhouse was assembled largely from scrapped dairy tanks and soft-drink equipment.
The brewery still uses whole-cone hops rather than pellets across its core beers.
Its Chico and Mills River sites run extensive solar and CO2-recovery systems; Mills River is LEED Platinum certified.

timeline.event: Brewery founded in Chico; first pale ale brewed
Mills River, North Carolina brewery opens

name: Bières de Chimay (Abbaye de Scourmont)
location: Abbaye Notre-Dame de Scourmont, Chimay, Belgium
description: Chimay, brewed at the Abbey of Notre-Dame de Scourmont in Belgium's Hainaut province, is the Trappist brewery that brought monastic beer to the world. Its color-coded range — Red, White (Cinq Cents), Blue and the newer Green (150) — plus abbey cheeses fund the monastery and extensive regional employment, making it the largest and most widely exported of the authentic Trappist producers.
history: Monks from Westvleteren founded Scourmont Abbey on a barren plateau near Chimay in 1850 and opened its brewery in 1862, the first to sell beer under the Trappist name. The modern era began after WWII: Father Théodore De Haene, working with Leuven brewing professor Jean De Clerck, isolated the abbey's house yeast in 1948 and reformulated the range, starting with the dark Christmas ale that became Chimay Blue. De Clerck's scientific rigor set standards the whole Trappist world adopted; he chose burial at the abbey in 1978. Production is now run by lay staff under monastic control through the Chimay Group, with bottling at nearby Baileux, and the 'Authentic Trappist Product' rules — brewing within the abbey, monastic oversight, profits to charity — codified partly in response to imitators.
facts: Chimay was the first brewery to market beer under the Trappist name.
The house yeast strain isolated in 1948 is still used across the range.
Chimay's cheeses, made with milk from local farms, predate many of its beers as an abbey industry.

timeline.event: Scourmont Abbey founded near Chimay
Abbey brewery begins selling beer
Father Théodore isolates the house yeast with Jean De Clerck

name: Russian River Brewing Company
location: Santa Rosa / Windsor, Sonoma County, California, USA
description: Russian River Brewing, in Sonoma County wine country, is a twin-engine cult brewery: on one side the hop bombs Pliny the Elder and Pliny the Younger, on the other a pioneering program of barrel-aged sour ales — Temptation, Supplication, Consecration — fermented with wild yeasts in wine barrels. Deliberately limited distribution and pub-first sales keep demand permanently ahead of supply.
history: The brewery was founded in 1997 by Korbel Champagne Cellars, which hired Vinnie Cilurzo — the Temecula brewer who had created the first commercial double IPA at Blind Pig in 1994. When Korbel exited beer in 2003, Vinnie and Natalie Cilurzo bought the brand, opening the downtown Santa Rosa brewpub in 2004. Pliny the Elder, first brewed for a 2000 festival, grew into America's most famous IPA, while Cilurzo's wine-country sour program helped invent American wild ale. The annual February release of triple IPA Pliny the Younger became a tourism event measured in millions of dollars for Sonoma County. A large Windsor brewery and taproom opened in 2018, funded partly by devoted fans, keeping the company fiercely independent.
facts: Vinnie Cilurzo brewed the first commercial double IPA at Blind Pig Brewing in 1994.
Pliny the Younger's two-week February release draws pilgrims from around the world to Sonoma County.
The brewery's sour program ferments in used wine barrels from neighboring Sonoma wineries.

timeline.event: Brewery founded by Korbel Champagne Cellars
Vinnie and Natalie Cilurzo buy the brewery
Windsor production brewery and taproom open

name: Brasserie d'Orval
description: The Orval brewery, inside a Trappist abbey in Belgium's far-southern Gaume region, makes exactly one beer for the public — the dry-hopped, Brettanomyces-conditioned pale ale that bears the abbey's name — plus cheese from the same monastic economy. Its singularity of purpose, art-deco bottle and living, evolving beer give it a status among brewers that far outstrips its size.
history: Orval Abbey dates to 1132, its name tied to the legend of Countess Matilda's ring returned by a trout from the spring — the image on every bottle. Ruined in the French Revolution's aftermath, the site was refounded from 1926, and the brewery was built in 1931 explicitly to finance reconstruction, an unusually commercial mandate that came with professional lay brewers from the start; architect Henry Vaes designed both the rebuilt abbey and the distinctive skittle-shaped bottle. The recipe — English-style dry hopping, hard water, mixed-culture bottle conditioning with Brettanomyces — has remained essentially intact. Anne-Françoise Pypaert, at the brewery since 1992, became the first female master brewer in the Trappist world in 2013 and later its director.
facts: One public beer, one cheese: the abbey's entire commercial output is deliberately minimal.
The skittle-shaped bottle and chalice were designed in the 1930s by the abbey's architect, Henry Vaes.
Fresh and year-aged Orval taste like different beers thanks to slow Brettanomyces refermentation.

timeline.event: Cistercian abbey established at Orval
Brewery founded to fund the abbey's reconstruction
Anne-Françoise Pypaert becomes the Trappist world's first female master brewer

name: Asahi Shuzo (Dassai)
description: Asahi Shuzo is the Yamaguchi sake house behind Dassai — not to be confused with the unrelated Asahi beer company or the Niigata brewery of similar name. It brews junmai daiginjo exclusively, from Yamada Nishiki rice, in a climate-controlled tower kura where salaried staff guided by fermentation data replace the traditional toji master-brewer system, brewing year-round for a global premium market.
history: Founded in 1948 in a mountain hamlet outside Iwakuni, Asahi Shuzo was a failing local brewery selling cheap sake when third-generation Hiroshi Sakurai took over in 1984. Facing collapse — and after losing the seasonal toji — he bet the company on ultra-premium junmai daiginjo, launching Dassai in 1990 and the record-setting Dassai 23 in 1992, with employees brewing from data instead of inherited intuition. Year-round brewing in a twelve-storey climate-controlled headquarters followed, along with obsessive investment in Yamada Nishiki rice supply. Son Kazuhiro Sakurai, now president, drove international expansion: Paris's Dassai Joël Robuchon café, a partnership culture with fine dining, and the Dassai Blue brewery in Hyde Park, New York, opened in 2023.
facts: The company abolished the toji system — brewing is run by salaried employees using daily analytical data.
It brews only junmai daiginjo, all from Yamada Nishiki rice.
Dassai Blue in Hyde Park, New York (2023) brews sake steps from the Culinary Institute of America.

timeline.event: Asahi Shuzo founded near Iwakuni, Yamaguchi
Hiroshi Sakurai takes over the struggling brewery
Dassai brand launched, junmai daiginjo only
Dassai Blue brewery opens in New York State

name: Hakkaisan Brewery
description: Hakkaisan brews beneath its namesake sacred mountain in Minamiuonuma, one of Japan's deepest snow countries, and embodies Niigata's tanrei-karakuchi ideal: sake that is light, dry, clean and built for the table. The company treats even its cheapest futsushu to ginjo-grade care, stores sake in a snow-cooled yukimuro warehouse, and has branched into beer, shochu and whisky at its Uonuma no Sato campus.
history: Founded in 1922 by the Nagumo family in a village at the foot of Mount Hakkai, the brewery drew on the region's advantages — months of snowpack, the ultra-soft Raiden-sama spring water, and winters cold enough for slow, stable fermentation. Postwar it grew from a local kura into one of Niigata's flagship names as the prefecture's dry, restrained style swept Japan in the 1980s sake booms. Rather than chase aroma competitions, Hakkaisan doubled down on drinkability and food affinity, codifying a philosophy of quality at scale. The third-generation president Jiro Nagumo built the Uonuma no Sato complex, revived snow-storage aging in a 1,000-tonne yukimuro in 2013, and pushed exports, sparkling sake and the Hakkaisan-backed whisky venture.
facts: The yukimuro snow room keeps sake at about 3 °C year-round using packed winter snow and no electricity.
Brewing water comes from the Raiden-sama spring, fed by Mount Hakkai's snowmelt.
Minamiuonuma is equally famous for Koshihikari rice — Japan's most prized table rice.

timeline.event: Hakkaisan founded at the foot of Mount Hakkai
Modern yukimuro snow-aging warehouse completed

name: Kikusui Sake Co.
description: Kikusui, founded in 1881 in Shibata on the Niigata plain, pairs approachable, widely exported premium sake with a streak of genuine innovation — most famously Funaguchi, the first commercially successful unpasteurized nama genshu in a can. Its junmai ginjo is many overseas drinkers' first premium sake, light and softly fruity in the prefecture's clean style.
history: The Takasawa family founded Kikusui — 'chrysanthemum water', from a noh legend of longevity-granting chrysanthemum dew — in 1881. The brewery endured floods and fire in its first decades, rebuilding each time, and grew through the twentieth century into one of Niigata's best-known producers. In 1972 it upended convention with Funaguchi Kikusui Ichiban Shibori: raw, undiluted, aggressively fresh sake stabilized in an aluminum can, a category first that remains a konbini icon in its gold livery. The company founded a sake culture research institute in 2004, collecting brewing documents and vessels, and pushed early into the US market, where its junmai ginjo became a sushi-bar standard.
facts: Funaguchi (1972) was the first mass-market canned nama genshu — unpasteurized, undiluted sake.
The name comes from a legend in which chrysanthemum dew grants long life.
Kikusui runs its own research institute dedicated to sake culture and brewing history.

timeline.event: Kikusui founded in Shibata, Niigata
Funaguchi canned nama genshu launched

name: HiteJinro
description: HiteJinro is South Korea's dominant drinks company and the maker of Jinro soju — the best-selling spirit brand on earth by volume. Formed from the union of Jinro (soju, founded 1924) and Hite (beer), it commands the Korean soju market with Chamisul and the retro-styled Jinro Is Back, exports to scores of countries, and effectively defines the green-bottle category worldwide.
history: Jinro began in 1924 as the Jincheon Yanghaeng brewery in Yonggang, in today's North Korea, adopting the toad mascot in 1955 after relocating south during the Korean War. Through decades of rationing-era reformulation — distilled rice soju gave way to diluted neutral-spirit soju after 1965 grain laws — Jinro became the national drink's defining maker, launching Chamisul in 1998 with bamboo-charcoal filtration and steadily falling ABVs. After a financial-crisis bankruptcy, beer maker Hite acquired Jinro in 2005; the merged HiteJinro took its current form in 2011. The company has topped Drinks International's global spirits-volume ranking for decades, selling on the order of ninety-plus million cases a year, and rode the Korean-wave export boom with fruit sojus and the revived 1970s-style Jinro toad branding.
facts: Jinro has ranked as the world's largest spirit brand by volume for decades running.
The toad mascot, adopted in 1955, is one of Korea's most recognizable trademarks.
Korean producers share a standardized green 360 ml bottle to enable industry-wide reuse.

timeline.event: Jinro founded as Jincheon Yanghaeng in Yonggang
Chamisul launched, resetting the modern soju market
Merged company takes the HiteJinro name

name: Hwayo (Kwangjuyo Group)
description: Hwayo is Korea's leading premium soju distillery, created by the Kwangjuyo ceramics house to prove that soju could be a craft spirit rather than a commodity. From its Yeoju distillery it makes 100% Korean-rice soju by low-temperature vacuum distillation, rested in breathable onggi earthenware, in strengths from an easy 17% to a cask-like 53% — with the 41 as flagship.
history: The Kwangjuyo Group, celebrated for reviving Joseon-dynasty ceramic craft, established the Hwayo venture in 2003 and launched its first spirits in 2005, explicitly reconnecting soju to the distilled rice tradition that South Korea's 1965 grain-rationing laws had all but erased in favor of diluted neutral spirit. The name fuses 'fire' and 'kiln', and the ceramics DNA is literal — maturation happens in onggi jars, and the bottles echo Kwangjuyo tableware. Hwayo built its reputation through Korean fine dining and duty-free retail, added an oak-aged expression (X.Premium) that flirts with whisky territory, and became the reference pour as premium soju and Korean cuisine rose together internationally.
facts: Hwayo grew out of Kwangjuyo, a ceramics house — its soju rests in traditional onggi clay jars.
Vacuum distillation at low temperature preserves delicate rice aromatics.
The range runs 17%, 25%, 41%, 53% and an oak-aged X.Premium.

timeline.event: Kwangjuyo Group establishes the Hwayo distillery venture
First Hwayo rice soju released

name: Angry Orchard Cider Company
location: Walden, New York, USA (brand of Boston Beer Co.)
description: Angry Orchard is the brand that rebuilt the American cider market. Launched nationally in 2011 by Boston Beer Company, it took the top spot within two years and has held a commanding share since, led by the semi-sweet Crisp Apple. A 60-acre Hudson Valley orchard and innovation cidery in Walden, New York gives the mass brand a genuine orchard research arm.
history: Boston Beer, maker of Samuel Adams, had sold HardCore cider since the 1990s, but reconceived the category for the craft era with Angry Orchard's 2011 national launch — gnarled-tree branding, beer-style packaging and an approachable sweet-tart profile timed perfectly for gluten-free and beyond-beer drinking. By 2013 it was the best-selling US cider, at times holding roughly half the market. In 2015 the company opened the Walden, New York cidery on a historic apple orchard, where cider makers run wild-fermentation, rosé, and single-orchard experiments far from the flagship's mass profile. The brand weathered the seltzer wave that battered cider broadly, remaining the category's anchor.
facts: Became America's best-selling cider within about two years of launch.
The Walden, NY innovation cidery sits on a 60-acre orchard dating to the early 1900s.
Bittersweet cider apples from European-style orchards join American culinary fruit in the blends.

timeline.event: Angry Orchard launches nationally
Becomes the top-selling cider in the United States
Innovation cidery opens in Walden, New York

name: Eric Bordelet
location: Château de Hauteville, Charchigné, France (Normandy–Maine borderlands)
description: Eric Bordelet is the sommelier-turned-cider-maker whose sidres and poirés from the Normandy–Maine borderlands convinced the wine world to take cider seriously. Farming dozens of heirloom apple and pear varieties organically on schist and granite, he bottles champagne-style cuvées — Sidre Tendre, Sidre Brut, the single-terroir Sydre Argelette and the ancient-tree Poiré Granit — that appear on Michelin-starred lists worldwide.
history: Bordelet spent the 1980s as a Paris sommelier, most notably at Alain Passard's three-star L'Arpège, before returning in 1992 to his family's farm at Charchigné on the southern fringe of Normandy, encouraged by winemaker friends (the late Didier Dagueneau among his admirers). He replanted and grafted toward a collection of heirloom cider apples and perry pears — bittersweet, bittersharp, acidic — farming organically with biodynamic leanings and mapping his parcels like crus. His decision to treat cider with a vigneron's vocabulary of terroir, vintage and cuvée hierarchy, plus champagne-format bottles refermenting under cork, created the template for prestige cider. Poiré Granit, from pear trees up to three centuries old on granite soils, became his most celebrated wine-list crossover.
facts: Bordelet was sommelier at Alain Passard's three-Michelin-star L'Arpège before making cider.
Poiré Granit comes from perry pear trees up to roughly 300 years old.
He farms dozens of heirloom apple and pear varieties organically on schist and granite soils.

timeline.event: Bordelet leaves Paris sommellerie to take over the family orchards at Charchigné
First estate sidre vintages produced

name: Château Margaux
description: One of the five First Growths of the 1855 classification and the only one sharing its name with its appellation, Château Margaux is the Médoc's byword for perfume and finesse. From roughly 80 hectares of red-wine vines on deep gravel croupes, the estate makes a cabernet-led grand vin celebrated for violet aromatics, silken tannin and decades of longevity, alongside Pavillon Rouge, Pavillon Blanc and a third wine.
history: The vineyard was assembled by the Lestonnac family in the late sixteenth century and refined over generations; the estate's neo-Palladian château, designed by Louis Combes, was completed in 1815. Ranked a Premier Grand Cru Classé in 1855, Margaux drifted through mid-twentieth-century mediocrity until Greek-French grocery magnate André Mentzelopoulos bought it in 1977 and spent heavily on vineyards and cellars. His daughter Corinne led a celebrated era with director Paul Pontallier from 1990 until Pontallier's death in 2016; Philippe Bascaules, who had spent two decades at the estate before running Inglenook in Napa, returned as managing director. A Norman Foster-designed winery, inaugurated with the 2015 vintage, brought the first major building works in two hundred years.
facts: Thomas Jefferson, visiting Bordeaux in 1787, listed Margaux among the region's four great estates.
The 1815 château is nicknamed the 'Versailles of the Médoc'.
Roughly two-thirds of the crop is declassified into second and third wines to protect the grand vin.

timeline.event: Classified a Premier Grand Cru Classé
André Mentzelopoulos acquires the estate
Norman Foster-designed cellar complex inaugurated

name: Opus One Winery
parentCompany: Constellation Brands & Baron Philippe de Rothschild S.A. (50/50)
description: Opus One is the Oakville estate born of a handshake between Napa's Robert Mondavi and Bordeaux's Baron Philippe de Rothschild — a single Bordeaux-blend red made each year to marry Napa fruit with First Growth discipline. Its crescent-shaped, partly buried winery is a valley landmark, and its distribution through the Bordeaux négociant system gives it a global reach few American wines match.
history: Robert Mondavi and Baron Philippe de Rothschild of Château Mouton Rothschild agreed to the joint venture during a 1978 meeting in Hawaii, and the first vintage was made in 1979 within Mondavi's To Kalon facility. The partnership was announced in 1980, the name Opus One chosen in 1982, and the debut 1979 and 1980 vintages released together in 1984. The purpose-built winery opened in 1991. Ownership followed the founders' successions — the Baroness Philippine de Rothschild on one side, and Constellation Brands after its 2004 purchase of the Mondavi company on the other — while the estate consolidated its four Oakville vineyard parcels and en primeur sales via the Place de Bordeaux from 2004 onward.
facts: The label bears the joined profiles of both founders and both signatures.
Overture, the second wine, is blended from declassified lots across multiple years.
Opus One farms roughly 68 hectares of tightly spaced Bordeaux varieties around the winery.

timeline.event: First vintage produced at the Robert Mondavi Winery
Debut vintages released at a record Napa price
Estate winery in Oakville completed
Constellation Brands succeeds Mondavi as 50% partner

name: Marchesi Antinori
location: Bargino, San Casciano Val di Pesa, Tuscany, Italy
description: The Antinori family has made wine in Tuscany for more than six centuries and 26 generations, making it one of the world's oldest family businesses. From its striking hillside winery at Bargino in Chianti Classico, the house spans estates across Italy and abroad, but its identity rests on the Super Tuscan revolution it ignited with Tignanello and Solaia — sangiovese and cabernet treated with uncompromising modern technique.
history: Giovanni di Piero Antinori enrolled in Florence's winemakers' guild, the Arte Fiorentina dei Vinattieri, in 1385, beginning an unbroken family line in the trade. The modern era belongs to Marchese Piero Antinori, who took charge in 1966 and, with oenologist Giacomo Tachis, created Tignanello (first solo vintage 1971) and Solaia (1978), wines that defied DOC rules and forced Italian wine law to modernize. The company expanded with estates in Bolgheri (Guado al Tasso), Umbria, Piedmont, Washington State and beyond, always family-owned. In 2012 the gravity-flow, earth-covered Antinori nel Chianti Classico winery opened at Bargino; Piero's daughter Albiera became president in 2017, with sisters Allegra and Alessia alongside and Renzo Cotarella as long-serving chief oenologist.
facts: 26 consecutive generations of the Antinori family have run the business since 1385.
House oenologists Giacomo Tachis and later Renzo Cotarella shaped the entire Super Tuscan category.
The Bargino headquarters winery is buried under a working vineyard, with a spiral staircase carved in corten steel.

timeline.event: Giovanni di Piero Antinori joins the Florentine winemakers' guild
Tignanello bottled as a stand-alone wine, launching the Super Tuscans
Antinori nel Chianti Classico winery opens at Bargino

name: Caymus Vineyards
description: Caymus is the Rutherford house of the Wagner family and the maker of America's most famous steakhouse cabernet. Its style — dark, ripe, plush and immediately generous — defined a commercial ideal for Napa Valley cabernet sauvignon, while the limited Special Selection bottling built its critical reputation. The family's broader portfolio spans Conundrum, Mer Soleil, Belle Glos and ventures beyond California.
history: Charlie Wagner, his wife Lorna Belle Glos Wagner and their son Chuck founded Caymus in 1972 on farmland in Rutherford, naming it for the Rancho Caymus land grant. The first vintage was made that year in modest quantities, and the flagship cabernet grew steadily on a reputation for consistency and approachability. Special Selection, introduced with the 1975 vintage, was twice named Wine Spectator's Wine of the Year — the only wine so honored. Chuck Wagner has led winemaking for decades, pioneering extended hang time for supple tannins, while his children launched their own labels within Wagner Family of Wine. The company has since expanded with vineyards in Suisun Valley and projects outside California, remaining wholly family-owned.
facts: Named for Rancho Caymus, the 1836 Mexican land grant covering the Rutherford area.
Caymus Special Selection is the only wine named Wine Spectator Wine of the Year twice.
Three generations of Wagners have made wine at the Rutherford property.

timeline.event: Caymus founded; first cabernet vintage produced
First Special Selection vintage

name: Cloudy Bay Vineyards
location: Jacksons Road, Blenheim, Marlborough, New Zealand
description: Cloudy Bay is the winery that made Marlborough — and New Zealand — famous. Its explosively aromatic sauvignon blanc became the international benchmark for the variety within a few vintages of its 1985 debut, defining a national style of passionfruit, cut grass and racy acidity. Now part of LVMH, it also produces chardonnay, pinot noir, the late-disgorged sparkling Pelorus and the oak-aged sauvignon Te Koko.
history: David Hohnen, founder of Cape Mentelle in Western Australia, established Cloudy Bay in 1985 after tasting early Marlborough sauvignon blancs — barely a decade after the region's first commercial vines went in (1973). Founding winemaker Kevin Judd made the debut vintage partly in borrowed space, and UK demand turned the label into a cult allocation by the late 1980s. Veuve Clicquot bought a majority stake in 1990, bringing the house fully into LVMH by 2003 and funding vineyard expansion across the Wairau Valley and a pinot noir outpost in Central Otago. Judd departed in 2009 to found Greywacke; the house style — precise, aromatic, screwcap-sealed — has remained deliberately consistent.
facts: Named for the bay at the Wairau River mouth charted by Captain James Cook in 1770.
One of the first five wineries established in Marlborough.
Founding winemaker Kevin Judd's vineyard photography shaped the brand's imagery for two decades.

timeline.event: Winery founded by David Hohnen; first sauvignon blanc vintage
Veuve Clicquot acquires a majority stake
Fully absorbed into LVMH's wine and spirits division

name: Weingut Dr. Loosen
description: Dr. Loosen is one of the Mosel's flagship riesling estates, farming steep slate vineyards in a string of legendary sites — Wehlener Sonnenuhr, Ürziger Würzgarten, Erdener Prälat among them — many planted with old, ungrafted vines. The house spans the full Prädikat spectrum from featherweight Kabinett to nobly sweet rarities, plus long-aged dry Grosses Gewächs bottlings, and its garrulous owner Ernst Loosen is German riesling's best-known global evangelist.
history: The estate at St. Johannishof has been in the Loosen family for over two hundred years, but its modern reputation dates to 1988, when Ernst 'Erni' Loosen — a trained archaeologist — reluctantly took over, slashed yields, abandoned fertilizers and refocused on the old vines in the family's grand-cru-class parcels. Recognition followed rapidly, including membership of the VDP growers' association and international acclaim. Loosen built parallel ventures without diluting the estate: the high-volume Dr. L rieslings from purchased Mosel fruit, the Eroica collaboration with Chateau Ste. Michelle in Washington State (from 1999), and projects in the Pfalz (Villa Wolf) and Oregon. Longtime cellar master Bernhard Schug shaped the winemaking alongside him for three decades.
facts: Holdings include ungrafted, pre-phylloxera riesling vines more than a century old.
Ernst Loosen co-created the Eroica riesling joint venture in Washington State in 1999.
The estate helped lead the revival of long-cask-aged dry Mosel riesling under the GG banner.

timeline.event: Ernst Loosen takes over the family estate
Eroica joint venture with Chateau Ste. Michelle launches

name: Château d'Esclans
description: Château d'Esclans, in the hills above the Esclans valley east of the Côtes de Provence, is the estate that turned pale Provençal rosé into a global luxury category. Its tiered range runs from the phenomenon Whispering Angel through Rock Angel and Les Clans to Garrus — an oak-fermented old-vine rosé priced like fine white burgundy — all built on grenache and the local rolle grape.
history: The site's winemaking history is ancient and its château nineteenth-century, but the modern story begins in 2006 when Sacha Lichine — son of Bordeaux author-négociant Alexis Lichine — sold Château Prieuré-Lichine in Margaux and bought d'Esclans to prove rosé could be serious. With the late Patrick Léon, former technical director of Mouton Rothschild, he built a range topped by Garrus from 80-plus-year-old vines and floated Whispering Angel as the accessible entry. American demand made it a cultural phenomenon. Moët Hennessy bought 55% in 2019 and later increased its stake, scaling Whispering Angel into one of the world's best-selling premium rosés while the estate wines kept the critical franchise.
facts: The name Whispering Angel comes from cherubs carved in the estate chapel.
Garrus, the top cuvée, ferments and ages entirely in large new oak demi-muids — almost unheard of for rosé.
Sacha Lichine sold a classified-growth Margaux estate to fund the rosé gamble.

timeline.event: Sacha Lichine acquires the estate and creates Whispering Angel
Moët Hennessy takes a 55% stake

name: Taylor's (Taylor, Fladgate & Yeatman)
description: Taylor's is one of the great names of vintage port and the anchor house of The Fladgate Partnership, with over three centuries of unbroken trade from its lodges in Vila Nova de Gaia. Its vintage ports from Quinta de Vargellas and Quinta de Terra Feita are among the category's most collected, while its aged tawny program and the invention of Late Bottled Vintage shaped how everyday drinkers meet port.
history: Founded in 1692 by Job Bearsley, the firm evolved through the names of successive partners — Taylor arriving in 1816, Fladgate in 1837, Yeatman in 1844 — while never leaving family-linked control or being absorbed by a drinks conglomerate. It bought the flagship Quinta de Vargellas estate in the remote upper Douro in 1893, rebuilding it after phylloxera. In 1970 Taylor's released the first commercial Late Bottled Vintage, democratizing vintage-style port. The modern Fladgate Partnership grew around it, acquiring Fonseca (long affiliated), Croft and Krohn, and diversifying into Porto hospitality with the World of Wine district. David Guimaraens, of the Fonseca family line, has led winemaking across the houses since the 1990s.
facts: More than 330 years old and still outside conglomerate ownership — rare among major port houses.
Released the first commercial Late Bottled Vintage port (1965 vintage, released 1970).
Quinta de Vargellas has its own railway halt in the upper Douro.

timeline.event: House founded in the Douro trade by Job Bearsley
Quinta de Vargellas acquired
First commercial LBV port released

name: González Byass
location: Jerez de la Frontera, Andalusia, Spain
description: González Byass is the Jerez bodega behind Tío Pepe, the world's best-known fino sherry, and a custodian of the region's most celebrated cellars — including the Gustave Eiffel-linked circular bodega La Concha. Family-owned into its fifth and sixth generations, it spans the full sherry spectrum from bone-dry finos through VORS-certified ancient soleras, plus brandy and a portfolio of Spanish still-wine estates.
history: Manuel María González Ángel founded the bodega in 1835 at age 23, mentored by his uncle José Ángel de la Peña — the original Tío Pepe, whose butt of pale dry fino became the house's calling card in the 1840s. London agent Robert Blake Byass became a partner in 1855, giving the firm its binational name and its grip on the British market; the Byass interest was bought out in 1988, returning the company wholly to the González family. The bodega complex grew into a city within a city — cathedral-scale aging halls, the famous sherry-drinking mice legend, and soleras holding wines well over a century old. Master blender Antonio Flores, born within the bodega walls, has become the modern face of fino.
facts: The giant illuminated Tío Pepe sign over Madrid's Puerta del Sol is a protected city landmark.
The circular La Concha bodega is traditionally associated with Gustave Eiffel's engineering office.
Cellar tradition includes setting out a tiny glass of sherry — with a ladder — for the bodega mice.

timeline.event: Manuel María González founds the bodega in Jerez
Robert Blake Byass becomes partner; firm renamed González Byass
González family buys out the Byass shareholding

name: Midleton Distillery
description: Midleton is the powerhouse of Irish whiskey — the County Cork complex where Irish Distillers produces Jameson, Redbreast, the Spot range, Powers and Midleton Very Rare. Its vast copper pots and column stills make both single pot still whiskey, Ireland's indigenous style built on malted and unmalted barley, and the grain whiskey behind the world's best-selling Irish blends, under master distiller Kevin O'Gorman.
history: The Murphy brothers converted a Midleton woollen mill into a distillery in 1825, and it grew into the leading house of Cork distilling, merging into Cork Distilleries Company in 1867. The pivotal moment came in 1966, when the remnants of the once-mighty Irish industry — Jameson, Powers and Cork Distilleries — merged as Irish Distillers to survive; in 1975 all production consolidated into the purpose-built New Midleton distillery beside the Victorian original. Pernod Ricard acquired Irish Distillers in 1988 and funded the export push that made Jameson a global phenomenon. Redbreast, the Spot whiskeys and Midleton Very Rare have since led the revival of single pot still whiskey, while the old distillery serves as a visitor centre.
facts: The old Midleton distillery houses one of the largest pot stills ever built, with a capacity of roughly 143,000 litres.
Virtually every major Irish Distillers whiskey — Jameson, Redbreast, the Spots, Powers — is distilled at Midleton.
The 1966 Irish Distillers merger is widely credited with saving Irish whiskey from extinction.
A micro-distillery on site produces the experimental Method and Madness range.

timeline.event: Murphy brothers found the Midleton distillery
Jameson, Powers and Cork Distilleries merge as Irish Distillers
New Midleton distillery opens; Dublin distilling winds down
Pernod Ricard acquires Irish Distillers

name: Old Bushmills Distillery
description: Bushmills is the historic malt whiskey house of Ireland's north, trading on a 1608 royal licence to distil in its territory — the basis of its claim to be the world's oldest licensed whiskey distillery. Uniquely among the old Irish houses it specialised in triple-distilled single malt rather than pot still blends, and its light, honeyed, fruit-driven style anchors both its single malts and the Black Bush and Original blends.
history: King James I granted Sir Thomas Phillips a licence to distil in the Bushmills territory in 1608; the Old Bushmills Distillery Company was registered in 1784. A fire destroyed the works in 1885, and the rebuilt Victorian distillery still operates beside the River Bush, drawing water from Saint Columb's Rill. The company pioneered transatlantic marketing — its steamship SS Bushmills carried whiskey to America in 1890 — and survived Prohibition and the Irish industry's collapse. Modern ownership has rotated through Irish Distillers (1972), Pernod Ricard, and Diageo (2005); since 2015 Bushmills has belonged to Proximo Spirits, acquired in the deal that gave Diageo full control of Don Julio tequila.
facts: The 1608 licence from King James I underpins the 'oldest licensed distillery' claim, though the company dates to 1784.
Its water source is Saint Columb's Rill, a tributary of the River Bush.
The company steamship SS Bushmills delivered whiskey directly to America from 1890.
Traded from Diageo to Proximo Spirits in 2015 in exchange for full ownership of Don Julio.

timeline.event: Royal licence to distil granted for the Bushmills territory
Old Bushmills Distillery Company registered
Distillery destroyed by fire and rebuilt
Proximo Spirits acquires Bushmills

name: Yamazaki Distillery
description: Yamazaki is Japan's first malt whisky distillery, built by Suntory founder Shinjiro Torii in 1923 in a humid, misty valley between Osaka and Kyoto famed for its water. Designed to produce many spirit styles under one roof — varied stills, mixed washbacks, multiple yeasts — it supplies Suntory's blends and the Yamazaki single malts, whose mizunara-oak character became the defining flavour of Japanese whisky's global rise.
history: Shinjiro Torii, a wine merchant turned drinks visionary, broke ground at Yamazaki in 1923 and hired Masataka Taketsuru — the chemist who had studied whisky-making in Scotland and later founded Nikka — as first distillery manager. The first Suntory whisky appeared in 1929, and decades of blend-focused production followed before the Yamazaki single malt launched in 1984. International recognition arrived with a 2003 ISC gold medal, and the subsequent boom in Japanese whisky demand emptied its aged warehouses, putting the 12- and 18-year-olds on permanent allocation. The distillery, extensively renovated in 2023 for its centenary, remains Japanese whisky's symbolic home.
facts: Japan's first malt whisky distillery, operating since 1923.
The valley's water was celebrated centuries before whisky — tea master Sen no Rikyu built a tea room nearby.
Runs eight pairs of pot stills in different shapes to create dozens of distillate styles on one site.
Pioneered maturation in Japanese mizunara oak, now the signature wood of Japanese whisky.

timeline.event: Construction begins on Japan's first malt whisky distillery
Suntory's first whisky, Shirofuda, is released
Yamazaki single malt launched
Centenary renovation completed

name: Hakushu Distillery
description: Hakushu is Suntory's mountain forest distillery, set at about 700 metres among woods at the foot of Mount Kaikomagatake in the Southern Japanese Alps. Granite-filtered snowmelt, cool high-altitude warehouses and a portion of lightly peated malt shape a house style of green freshness — mint, young fruit and soft smoke — that made its single malts the defining whisky of the Japanese highball.
history: Suntory opened Hakushu in 1973, fifty years after breaking ground at Yamazaki, to diversify its malt supply with a contrasting mountain terroir. Expansion through the late 1970s and 1980s — at one point the complex ran dozens of stills — made it among the largest malt distilleries in the world before capacity was rationalised around today's flexible stillhouse. Its single malts rose to prominence in the 1990s and 2000s, and the 12-year-old's 2018 suspension for lack of aged stock, followed by a 2021 return, became emblematic of the Japanese whisky shortage. The forested grounds, which include a bird sanctuary, share their water with one of Japan's best-known bottled mineral waters.
facts: Opened in 1973 on the fiftieth anniversary of Yamazaki's construction.
Sits at roughly 700 metres elevation in forest at the foot of the Southern Japanese Alps.
The grounds double as a certified wild-bird sanctuary.
At its 1980s peak it ranked among the largest malt whisky distilleries in the world.

timeline.event: Hakushu distillery opens in Yamanashi Prefecture
Hakushu 12 suspended amid aged-stock shortages
Hakushu 12 returns on allocation

name: Suntory
description: Suntory is the house that built Japanese whisky: founder Shinjiro Torii opened the country's first malt distillery at Yamazaki in 1923, and the company's blending tradition — today led by fifth-generation chief blender Shinji Fukuyo — spans Yamazaki, Hakushu and the Chita grain distillery. Its Hibiki blends and single malts define the elegant, harmony-first Japanese style, and its 2014 purchase of Beam made it one of the world's largest spirits groups.
history: Shinjiro Torii founded his Osaka drinks shop, Torii Shoten, in 1899 and funded his whisky ambitions with the hit Akadama Port Wine. Against prevailing advice he built Yamazaki in 1923, released Japan's first true whisky in 1929, and found the national palate with the softer Kakubin in 1937. The company, renamed Suntory, added the Chita grain distillery and Hakushu in the early 1970s as highballs made whisky Japan's everyday drink, and launched Hibiki for its ninetieth anniversary in 1989. International awards from the early 2000s ignited global demand that still outruns its aged stocks. In 2014 Suntory acquired Beam Inc. for about 16 billion dollars, creating Beam Suntory — now Suntory Global Spirits.
facts: Founder Shinjiro Torii's motto 'Yatte minahare' — roughly, 'Go for it and see' — remains company lore.
The Akadama Port Wine fortune financed Japan's first malt whisky distillery.
Chief blender Shinji Fukuyo is only the fifth person to hold the role in a century.
Bought Beam Inc. for roughly $16 billion in 2014, adding Jim Beam, Maker's Mark and Laphroaig to the group.

timeline.event: Shinjiro Torii founds Torii Shoten in Osaka
Japan's first whisky, Suntory Shirofuda, released
Hibiki launched for the company's 90th anniversary
Acquisition of Beam Inc. creates Beam Suntory

name: Nikka Whisky
location: Tokyo, Japan (distilleries: Yoichi, Hokkaidō and Miyagikyo, Sendai)
description: Nikka is the second pillar of Japanese whisky, founded by Masataka Taketsuru — the chemist who learned distilling in Scotland and is remembered as the father of Japanese whisky. Its two malt distilleries bracket the craft: coal-fired, muscular Yoichi on Hokkaido and orchard-elegant Miyagikyo near Sendai, joined by antique Coffey stills whose grain whiskies underpin blends like the cult From the Barrel.
history: Masataka Taketsuru travelled to Scotland in 1918, studied chemistry in Glasgow and apprenticed at distilleries including Hazelburn in Campbeltown, returning with a Scottish wife, Rita, and a notebook that became Japanese whisky's founding document. After a decade running Yamazaki for Suntory's predecessor, he struck out alone in 1934, founding Dai Nippon Kaju — later Nikka — at Yoichi on Hokkaido, chosen for its Scotland-like climate, and selling apple juice while his first whisky matured. Miyagikyo followed in 1969, Coffey stills were imported in the 1960s, and From the Barrel appeared in 1985. Nikka became part of Asahi's group in the post-war decades and a full subsidiary in 2001, and global demand since the 2010s has kept much of its range allocated.
facts: Founder Masataka Taketsuru apprenticed in Scottish distilleries, including Hazelburn in Campbeltown, in 1919–20.
The company's first name, Dai Nippon Kaju ('Great Japan Juice'), reflected the apple juice it sold while whisky aged.
Yoichi still direct-fires some stills with coal, a practice nearly extinct worldwide.
Taketsuru and his Scottish wife Rita were dramatised in the hit 2014–15 NHK television series Massan.

timeline.event: Taketsuru founds the company and builds Yoichi distillery on Hokkaido
First Nikka whisky released
Miyagikyo distillery opens near Sendai
Nikka becomes a wholly owned Asahi subsidiary

name: Kavalan Distillery
description: Kavalan is Taiwan's first whisky distillery and the flagbearer of subtropical maturation: Yilan County's heat and humidity age whisky several times faster than Scotland's climate, at the cost of a double-digit angel's share. Guided in its design by maturation scientist Dr Jim Swan, it vaulted from first spirit in 2006 to World's Best Single Malt in under a decade, and its cask-strength Solist bottlings are collected worldwide.
history: Beverage conglomerate King Car Group began building Kavalan in 2005 on the rainy Yilan plain, naming it for the region's indigenous people and hiring consultant Dr Jim Swan to engineer maturation for the tropics, including his signature shaved-toasted-recharred wine casks. The first spirit ran in March 2006 and the first bottles sold in December 2008. Validation came fast: a Kavalan topped a 2010 Burns Night blind tasting in Scotland, and in 2015 Solist Vinho Barrique was named World's Best Single Malt at the World Whiskies Awards. Now with one of the largest single-malt capacities in Asia, Kavalan exports to over sixty countries and has made 'Taiwanese whisky' a fixture of the world stage.
facts: Named for the Kavalan indigenous people of the Yilan plain.
Went from pouring its first spirit in 2006 to a World's Best Single Malt award in 2015.
Subtropical heat drives an angel's share of ten per cent or more per year, several times Scotland's rate.
Sister company King Car also produces Mr. Brown canned coffee, Taiwan's ubiquitous roadside brew.

timeline.event: King Car Group begins construction in Yilan County
First spirit distilled in March
First bottles go on sale in December
Solist Vinho Barrique named World's Best Single Malt (World Whiskies Awards)

name: Amrut Distilleries
description: Amrut is the pioneer of Indian single malt, a family-owned Bengaluru distillery that proved barley whisky matured at 3,000 feet in the tropics could stand beside Scotland's best. Its malts age roughly three times faster than Scotch — with an angel's share above ten per cent a year — and its Fusion flagship, marrying Indian and peated Scottish barley, ranks among the most acclaimed whiskies of the New World.
history: J.N. Radhakrishna Rao Jagdale founded Amrut Distilleries in 1948, the year after Indian independence, and the family firm spent decades producing spirits for the domestic market — including whisky blends and rum — before experimenting with single malt from Indian barley in the 1980s. Convinced that home-market prestige required foreign validation, the Jagdales launched Amrut Single Malt in Glasgow in 2004. Recognition snowballed after Jim Murray's Whisky Bible named Fusion the world's third-finest whisky in its 2010 edition, and Amrut's limited releases now sell out globally, with the distillery exporting to dozens of countries while remaining family-controlled.
facts: Amrut derives from the Sanskrit amrita, the nectar of immortality churned from the cosmic ocean in Hindu myth.
Launched its single malt in Glasgow in 2004 on the theory that convincing the Scots would convince everyone.
Bengaluru's elevation and climate mature whisky roughly three times faster than Scotland's.
Remains owned and run by the founding Jagdale family.

timeline.event: Amrut Distilleries founded in Bengaluru
Amrut Single Malt launched in Glasgow
Amrut Fusion released
Fusion ranked third-finest whisky in the world by the Whisky Bible

name: Eimverk Distillery
description: Eimverk is Iceland's pioneering whisky distillery, a family-run operation outside Reykjavík committed to 100% Icelandic ingredients. Its hardy subarctic barley — grown without pesticides and yielding far less sugar than commercial malt — gives the Flóki whiskies their earthy, cereal-forward character, while sister spirits Vor gin and Víti brennivín extend the all-Icelandic idea, and special editions revive sheep-dung smoking, the island's traditional curing method.
history: The Thorkelsson family founded Eimverk in 2009 with the aim of making whisky entirely from Icelandic grain, spending years and more than a hundred trial distillations adapting mashing and fermentation to low-sugar northern barley. Production began in earnest in 2012–13, and the distillery bridged its maturation wait with Flóki Young Malt in 2014 before releasing Iceland's first fully matured single malt in late 2017. Named for Hrafna-Flóki, the Norse explorer who named Iceland, the Flóki range has since grown to include sherry-cask and sheep-dung-smoked reserves, all produced in small batches at the Garðabær site.
facts: Iceland's first whisky distillery, founded by the Thorkelsson family in 2009.
Uses only Icelandic barley, which needs no pesticides at 64°N but yields so little sugar that each bottle takes ~50% more grain.
Smoked editions kiln barley over sheep dung, the traditional Icelandic fuel used to cure hangikjöt.
Also produces Vor gin and Víti brennivín from the same all-Icelandic philosophy.

timeline.event: Eimverk founded in Garðabær by the Thorkelsson family
Flóki Young Malt pre-release bridges the maturation wait
Iceland's first fully matured single malt whisky released

name: Glenfiddich Distillery
description: Glenfiddich is the best-selling single malt Scotch in the world and the distillery most responsible for the category's existence, having begun marketing single malt internationally in 1963. Still owned by the founding Grant family, it runs one of Scotland's largest malt operations from Dufftown while keeping its house style light, pear-fresh and approachable — the definitive gentle Speyside profile served in a famous triangular bottle.
history: William Grant, a former bookkeeper at Mortlach, built Glenfiddich in 1886–87 with the help of his nine children, using second-hand stills bought for £120; the first spirit ran on Christmas Day 1887. The distillery stayed in family hands through the twentieth century's consolidations, a rarity in Scotch. Facing a takeover-threatened blend market, the Grants made the pivotal decision in 1963 to promote Glenfiddich as a single ('straight') malt beyond Scotland — effectively inventing the modern single malt category — and in 1969 opened Scotland's first distillery visitor centre. Today William Grant & Sons remains independent, with Glenfiddich shipping over a million cases a year worldwide.
facts: Glenfiddich means 'valley of the deer' in Gaelic, the origin of the stag emblem.
William Grant built the distillery largely by hand with his nine children, using stills bought second-hand for £120.
Opened Scotland's first distillery visitor centre in 1969.
One of very few major Scotch distilleries still owned by its founding family.

timeline.event: First spirit runs on Christmas Day
Begins marketing single malt internationally, creating the modern category
Opens Scotland's first distillery visitor centre
12-year-old becomes the flagship expression

name: The Macallan Distillery
description: The Macallan is Scotch whisky's foremost luxury house, defined by an obsessive sherry-cask programme — it commissions its own oak, seasoned with Oloroso in Jerez — small spirit stills, natural colour and auction results that dwarf every rival. Its Speyside estate whiskies are rich, fruit-laden and polished, and the brand's prestige pricing has reshaped how the industry thinks about whisky as a luxury good.
history: Alexander Reid licensed The Macallan in 1824 among the first wave of legal Speyside distilleries, on farmland surrounding the 1700 Easter Elchies manor house above the Spey. It supplied blenders for over a century before Highland Distillers — later the Edrington Group — built its single malt into a global luxury brand from the 1980s, anchored by uncompromising sherry-oak maturation. In 2018 The Macallan opened a £140 million distillery cut into the hillside beneath an undulating turf roof, one of the most expensive ever built. Its old bottlings dominate the auction market: a 1926 cask yielded the two most expensive bottles of whisky ever sold, the record standing at about £2.19 million since 2023.
facts: Commissions its own casks — oak grown, coopered and sherry-seasoned in Spain to the distillery's specification.
Never adds caramel colouring; every hue comes from the wood.
The 2018 distillery, by architects Rogers Stirk Harbour + Partners, cost around £140 million.
Bottles from its 1926 cask have twice set the world auction record for whisky.

timeline.event: Alexander Reid licenses the distillery at Easter Elchies
Fine Oak range introduces bourbon-cask maturation alongside Sherry Oak
New turf-roofed distillery opens
A 1926 Macallan sells for ~£2.19 m, the standing world record

name: Lagavulin Distillery
location: Lagavulin Bay, near Port Ellen, Islay, Scotland
description: Lagavulin distils the most celebrated of Islay's south-shore peat giants from a small bay beneath the ruins of Dunyvaig Castle. Its house style is smoke with gravitas — slow-distilled, thickly peated spirit matured long enough to fold tar and iodine into dried fruit and leather. The 16-year-old flagship has anchored Diageo's Classic Malts since 1988 and remains the benchmark for mature peated whisky.
history: John Johnston licensed Lagavulin in 1816 on a bay where illicit stills had reportedly operated since the 1740s. Under Peter Mackie — one of Victorian whisky's great characters — it powered the White Horse blend from 1890 and hosted his eccentric experiments, including the short-lived Malt Mill distillery built within its walls in 1908. Chosen in 1988 to represent Islay in the Classic Malts range, Lagavulin 16 became a global icon of peated whisky. The distillery marked its bicentenary in 2016 with commemorative releases, and its partnership with actor Nick Offerman has kept it lodged in popular culture.
facts: Claims the slowest distillation on Islay, with a spirit run of around nine hours.
The legendary Malt Mill distillery, fictional grail of the film The Angels' Share, operated inside Lagavulin from 1908 to 1962.
Sits opposite the ruins of Dunyvaig Castle, once a naval fortress of the Lords of the Isles.
Heart of the White Horse blend from 1890.

timeline.event: John Johnston licenses the distillery
Peter Mackie launches the White Horse blend around Lagavulin malt
Selected for the Classic Malts range
Bicentenary celebrated with special releases

name: Ardbeg Distillery
description: Ardbeg makes the most heavily peated of Islay's classic malts yet distils it to an improbable brightness, using a purifier-equipped spirit still that keeps citrus and orchard fruit alive beneath 50-plus ppm of smoke. Rescued from dereliction in 1997, it has become one of whisky's great cult brands, feeding a devoted global Committee with the flagship Ten and a stream of playfully named limited releases.
history: Founded by John Macdougall in 1815 on the Kildalton coast, Ardbeg spent most of its life as a blender's malt and nearly did not survive the twentieth century: it closed in 1981, limped through partial production from 1989 and stood silent again by the mid-1990s. Glenmorangie plc bought the ruin in 1997 for about £7 million, restored it, and launched the definitive Ten Year Old in 2000 alongside the Ardbeg Committee fan organisation. LVMH's 2004 acquisition of Glenmorangie brought luxury-house backing, and under whisky creator Dr Bill Lumsden Ardbeg has ranged from space-flown maturation experiments to committee-only bottlings, while the core Ten remains its calling card.
facts: Sent new-make spirit and oak to the International Space Station in 2011 to study maturation in microgravity.
Bought back from dereliction in 1997 for roughly £7 million.
The Ardbeg Committee counts well over one hundred thousand members worldwide.
Its malt is peated to roughly 50–55 ppm, among the highest of any core-range Scotch.

timeline.event: John Macdougall founds Ardbeg
Distillery closes
Glenmorangie plc buys and revives the site
LVMH acquires Glenmorangie plc, including Ardbeg

name: Laphroaig Distillery
description: Laphroaig is the most medicinal of the Islay malts — iodine, seaweed and antiseptic smoke are its calling cards — and it remains one of the few Scotch distilleries still malting part of its own barley on traditional floors. Deliberately divisive and proud of it, the distillery balances its tarry phenolics with sweetness drawn from first-fill ex-bourbon wood, much of it from stablemate Maker's Mark.
history: Brothers Donald and Alexander Johnston established Laphroaig around 1815 beside a rocky cove on Islay's south coast. The founding family's line ended with Ian Hunter, who bequeathed the distillery in 1954 to his manager Bessie Williamson — the first woman to own and run a Scotch whisky distillery in the twentieth century. During Prohibition its whisky reportedly entered America legally as medicinal spirit. The Prince of Wales granted Laphroaig his Royal Warrant during a 1994 visit, the same year the Friends of Laphroaig scheme began leasing every member a square foot of Islay. Ownership passed through Allied and Fortune Brands to Beam, and with Suntory's 2014 acquisition Laphroaig joined the Japanese group now called Suntory Global Spirits.
facts: One of the few Scotch distilleries retaining working floor maltings, cold-smoking its barley over Islay peat.
Holds a Royal Warrant from the Prince of Wales, granted in 1994.
Bessie Williamson, owner from 1954, was the first woman to own and manage a Scotch distillery in the twentieth century.
Friends of Laphroaig members hold lifetime leases on individual square feet of Islay, rent payable in drams.

timeline.event: Johnston brothers establish Laphroaig
Bessie Williamson inherits the distillery
Royal Warrant granted; Friends of Laphroaig launched
Suntory acquires Beam Inc., bringing Laphroaig into the group

name: Talisker Distillery
description: Talisker is the historic distillery of the Isle of Skye, producing a maritime single malt of medium peat, sea salt and signature black-pepper fire from the shore of Loch Harport. Old-fashioned worm tub condensers and purifier-piped wash stills preserve a muscular spirit character, and its 'Made by the Sea' identity is among the most literal in whisky — storms genuinely batter the warehouse walls.
history: Brothers Hugh and Kenneth MacAskill founded Talisker in 1830 at Carbost on Skye's western shore. Robert Louis Stevenson counted it among Scotland's finest drinks in an 1880 poem, by which time the distillery had passed through the partnerships that would fold it into the Distillers Company. Triple distillation was practised until 1928, and a 1960 stillhouse fire destroyed all five stills, which were rebuilt as exact replicas to protect the spirit's character. Chosen for the Classic Malts in 1988, Talisker was Skye's only distillery for 187 years until Torabhaig opened in 2017, and it remains one of Diageo's flagship malts.
facts: Skye's only distillery from 1830 until 2017.
Condenses its spirit in outdoor worm tubs, a rarity that adds weight and character.
The five stills are exact replicas built after a 1960 stillhouse fire.
Praised in verse by Robert Louis Stevenson in 1880.

timeline.event: MacAskill brothers found Talisker at Carbost
Triple distillation abandoned for conventional double distillation
Stillhouse fire; stills rebuilt as exact replicas
Selected for the Classic Malts range

name: Highland Park Distillery
description: Highland Park is Scotland's northernmost distillery, working on Orkney since 1798 with hand-turned floor maltings and heather-rich local peat that yields aromatic, floral smoke unlike Islay's tar. Married with sherry-seasoned oak and bottled at natural colour, its malts balance honeyed sweetness and gentle smoke — a completeness that has made the 12-year-old a perennial critics' favourite — under branding that embraces Orkney's Norse past.
history: Distilling on the site above Kirkwall is credited to Magnus Eunson from 1798 — a church officer and part-time smuggler who, in distillery lore, hid whisky beneath his pulpit — with formal licensing following in 1826. Highland Park prospered supplying blends before Highland Distillers, later Edrington, developed its single malt through the late twentieth century into a core of the premium island category. The distillery maintains its floor maltings and Hobbister Moor peat cutting to this day. In 2017 the range was relaunched around Orkney's Viking heritage — the islands were Norse for over five centuries — renaming the 12-year-old Viking Honour.
facts: The most northerly Scotch whisky distillery, at nearly 59°N in Orkney.
Malts about a fifth of its barley on traditional floors and kilns it over heather-rich Hobbister peat.
Founder Magnus Eunson was simultaneously a church beadle and a whisky smuggler.
Orkney was ruled by Norway until 1468, the basis of the brand's Viking identity.

timeline.event: Magnus Eunson begins distilling on the site
Distillery formally licensed
Range relaunched with Viking-themed names and packaging

name: Springbank Distillery
description: Springbank is Scotch whisky's great traditionalist: the only distillery in Scotland to perform every step — floor malting all its barley, distilling, maturing and bottling — on one site, largely by hand. It produces three distinct whiskies (lightly peated Springbank, heavily peated Longrow, unpeated triple-distilled Hazelburn) in tiny volumes, and its oily, briny, faintly funky Campbeltown style has earned a cult following that outstrips supply.
history: Founded in 1828 on the site of an illicit still, Springbank has been controlled by the Mitchell family and their firm J & A Mitchell & Co for most of its existence, making it Scotland's oldest independent family-owned distillery. It survived the catastrophic 1920s collapse that reduced Campbeltown — once self-styled whisky capital of the world with over thirty distilleries — to just two working sites. The family bought the venerable bottler William Cadenhead in 1972 and revived the neighbouring Glengyle distillery in 2004, restoring Campbeltown's quorum of three distilleries and helping preserve its status as a recognised Scotch whisky region. Springbank still refuses chill filtration, colouring and, largely, automation.
facts: The only Scottish distillery to carry out 100% of production, from floor malting to bottling, on one site.
Makes three whiskies on the same stills: Springbank (2.5 distillations), Longrow (peated, double) and Hazelburn (unpeated, triple).
Owner J & A Mitchell & Co is Scotland's oldest independent family-owned distilling company.
The family's 2004 revival of Glengyle kept Campbeltown alive as an official whisky region.

timeline.event: Springbank founded on a former illicit still site
Mitchell family acquires independent bottler William Cadenhead
Sister distillery Glengyle revived

name: Johnnie Walker
description: Johnnie Walker is the world's best-selling Scotch whisky, a blending house whose colour-coded labels — Red through Blue — span every price tier and whose square bottle, slanted label and Striding Man logo are among the most recognised trademarks in drinks. Master blender Dr Emma Walker's team draws on malt and grain whiskies from across Diageo's Scottish distilleries, with smoky island malts a house signature.
history: John Walker sold whisky in his Kilmarnock grocery from 1820, but the brand's architecture was built by his son Alexander and grandsons: the square bottle came in 1860, the flagship blends took shape as Old Highland Whisky, and in 1908–09 the Striding Man was sketched and the colour names adopted. Walker's rode the blended Scotch boom to global dominance, merging into the Distillers Company in 1925 and eventually into Diageo. The brand now sells well over twenty million cases a year across roughly 180 countries. In 2021 Diageo opened Johnnie Walker Princes Street, an eight-storey visitor attraction in Edinburgh, as the brand's public home.
facts: The best-selling Scotch whisky in the world, at well over twenty million cases a year.
The Striding Man was drawn by cartoonist Tom Browne in 1908, reputedly on the back of a menu.
Labels have been applied at a 24-degree slant since the nineteenth century.
Dr Emma Walker, appointed 2022, is the first woman to serve as Johnnie Walker master blender — and no relation to the founder.

timeline.event: John Walker begins trading in Kilmarnock
Square bottle introduced
Red and Black Label colour names adopted
Johnnie Walker Princes Street opens in Edinburgh
